children.name: Rhinolophus
children.description: Horseshoe bats — named for the elaborate, horseshoe-shaped noseleaf that surrounds their nostrils and acts as a megaphone to focus echolocation calls emitted through the nose rather than the mouth. This allows them to echolocate while chewing prey, and enables extraordinary precision: they can detect a wire 0.08 mm thick in total darkness. Horseshoe bats are constant-frequency echolocators, emitting long calls at a fixed frequency and processing the Doppler shift in the returning echo to detect wing-beat patterns of flying insects. They use Doppler compensation — adjusting the emitted frequency to keep the returning echo at exactly the right pitch. All horseshoe bat species are threatened by declining insect populations and cave disturbance.

children.name: Rhinolophus ferrumequinum
children.description: One of the largest European bats; wingspan up to 40 cm. Population declined over 90% in Britain during the 20th century; now slowly recovering under intense conservation effort. Roosts in limestone caves in winter and old farm buildings in summer.

children.name: Rhinolophus hipposideros
children.description: Europe's smallest horseshoe bat, about the size of a large bumblebee. Wraps its wings around itself when roosting, resembling a small furry pear. Has recovered in Wales and Ireland following conservation of old stone buildings.

children.name: Rhinolophus euryale
children.description: A colonial cave-roosting bat of Mediterranean Europe and the Middle East. Forms maternity colonies of hundreds to thousands in limestone caves. Calls at around 106 kHz — too high for most bat detectors to capture.

children.name: Rhinolophus affinis
children.description: A widely distributed Asian species and one of the closest known relatives of bat coronaviruses related to SARS-CoV-2. Subject to intense virological study since 2020 to understand the origins of the pandemic virus.

children.name: Rhinolophus alcyone
children.description: A small West and Central African bat found in moist lowland forest and forest edges. Emits constant-frequency calls in the 90–95 kHz range to detect moths and other soft-bodied insects in dense vegetation. Roosts in small groups in caves and rock crevices.

children.name: Rhinolophus blasii
children.description: A medium-sized horseshoe bat distributed from southern Europe and North Africa east to Iran and the Caucasus. Roosts colonially in caves, often sharing roost sites with other Rhinolophus species. Named after the German anatomist Johann Heinrich Blasius.

children.name: Rhinolophus capensis
children.description: Endemic to the fynbos and renosterveld biomes of South Africa's Western Cape. Roosts in caves and rock crevices and forages in dense shrubland for small insects. Echolocates at 83–88 kHz, one of the lower frequencies in the African rhinolophid fauna.

children.name: Rhinolophus clivosus
children.description: A widespread bat of sub-Saharan Africa, North Africa, and the Arabian Peninsula, occupying savanna, bushveld, and rocky outcrops. Uses caves and disused mines as roost sites and maternity colonies can number in the thousands. Calls at approximately 96 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus cohenae
children.description: A recently described species from southern Africa, genetically and morphologically distinct from the closely related R. clivosus complex. Restricted to rocky habitats in Zimbabwe and South Africa. Its echolocation frequency is slightly higher than related species at around 100 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus darlingi
children.description: A medium-sized horseshoe bat of eastern and southern Africa, found in miombo woodland, bushveld, and savanna. Roosts in caves and mines in colonies of up to several hundred. Echolocates at approximately 83 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus denti
children.description: One of the smallest African horseshoe bats, found in arid and semi-arid zones of southern Africa and Angola. Roosts in narrow rock crevices and is rarely recorded due to its secretive habits and high echolocation frequency near 108 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus deckenii
children.description: An East African species associated with coastal and lowland forest in Kenya and Tanzania. Relatively little-studied; most records come from limestone cave surveys along the Kenyan and Tanzanian coast. Echolocates at around 79 kHz.
children.namedAfter: Baron Carl Claus von der Decken

children.name: Rhinolophus eloquens
children.description: A large East African horseshoe bat found in highland forests from Ethiopia south to Kenya and Uganda. Calls at approximately 57 kHz — one of the lowest frequencies recorded in the genus. Its large size and low-frequency calls allow it to detect large moths in forested highlands.

children.name: Rhinolophus fumigatus
children.description: A large, widespread savanna bat distributed across sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Somalia and south to South Africa. Roosts in caves, rock overhangs, and hollow trees. Echolocates at 46–52 kHz, the lowest frequency in the African Rhinolophus fauna, suited to detecting large insects.

children.name: Rhinolophus guineensis
children.description: Known from a small number of specimens from Guinea and Sierra Leone in West Africa. A poorly known species of moist lowland forest; its natural history and echolocation characteristics remain largely unstudied. Considered Data Deficient by the IUCN.

children.name: Rhinolophus hildebrandtii
children.description: A large East and southern African horseshoe bat that roosts colonially in caves and lava tubes. One of the larger African species, with a wingspan exceeding 38 cm. Echolocates at around 52 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus landeri
children.description: A small West and Central African bat widespread in savanna and forest-savanna mosaic habitats. Roosts in caves and termite mounds. Echolocates at approximately 107–115 kHz, one of the highest frequencies in African Rhinolophus.

children.name: Rhinolophus lobatus
children.description: A large East African horseshoe bat sometimes treated as a subspecies of R. hildebrandtii. Found in highland forests and savanna woodland of Ethiopia, Kenya, and Tanzania. Its noseleaf sella has a distinctive peaked profile used in species identification.

children.name: Rhinolophus maclaudi
children.description: A large but poorly known horseshoe bat from highland Guinea and surrounding West African countries. Known from only a handful of specimens; its roost sites, diet, and echolocation have not been formally documented. Listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN.

children.name: Rhinolophus maendeleo
children.description: Described in 2016 from the Albertine Rift in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, its name means 'development' or 'progress' in Swahili. Known only from a few specimens collected at forest-edge habitats near Kahuzi-Biéga National Park. Its echolocation and full range remain poorly studied.

children.name: Rhinolophus mossambicus
children.description: A small horseshoe bat of coastal and lowland eastern Africa, closely related to R. swinnyi. Roosts in coastal caves and rock overhangs in Mozambique and Tanzania. Echolocates at approximately 104–108 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus ruwenzorii
children.description: A rare montane bat restricted to the Ruwenzori Mountains on the border of Uganda and the Democratic Republic of Congo. Found at elevations of 1,500–2,500 m in Afromontane forest. Very few specimens are known and its biology remains largely undescribed.

children.name: Rhinolophus simulator
children.description: A medium-sized bat of wooded savanna in sub-Saharan Africa, from Kenya south to South Africa. Forages in dense woodland using its 80–84 kHz echolocation to detect moths resting on leaves. Roosts in caves and rock overhangs in small colonies.

children.name: Rhinolophus swinnyi
children.description: A small southern African horseshoe bat found in subtropical forest and bushveld from South Africa to Tanzania. Echolocates at 100–108 kHz, enabling it to detect small insects among foliage. Often caught in mist nets over forest streams.

children.name: Rhinolophus ziama
children.description: Described from the Ziama Forest Reserve in Guinea, one of West Africa's most biodiverse highland forests. Known from very few specimens; its relationship to other West African rhinolophids requires further molecular study. Listed as Data Deficient by the IUCN.

children.name: Rhinolophus acuminatus
children.description: A small bat of Southeast Asia found in Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Often roosts in limestone caves alongside other Rhinolophus species. Echolocates at approximately 83 kHz and forages in primary and secondary lowland forest.

children.name: Rhinolophus anderseni
children.description: An uncommon bat known from the Philippines and Borneo. Named after the Danish zoologist Knud Andersen, a pioneer of chiropteran systematics. Found in lowland and montane forest, roosting in small groups in caves and rock shelters.

children.name: Rhinolophus beddomei
children.description: An Indian endemic found in peninsular India and Sri Lanka, roosting in caves, mines, and old buildings. Echolocates at approximately 110 kHz, one of the higher frequencies in the Indian Rhinolophus fauna. Named after Colonel Richard Henry Beddome, a British-Indian naturalist.

children.name: Rhinolophus borneensis
children.description: Endemic to Borneo and adjacent small islands, this bat inhabits lowland and foothill forest up to about 1,200 m elevation. Roosts in limestone caves in small to moderate colonies. Its echolocation frequency is around 93–98 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus celebensis
children.description: Endemic to Sulawesi and the Moluccas in Indonesia, one of several Rhinolophus species unique to Wallacea. Inhabits lowland and montane tropical forest, roosting in caves and hollow trees. Its isolation on Sulawesi has produced morphological differences from related Sundaland species.

children.name: Rhinolophus cognatus
children.description: Endemic to the Andaman Islands in the Indian Ocean, this species has been isolated from mainland populations long enough to be recognized as distinct. Roosts in limestone caves and inhabits island rainforest. Its exact echolocation frequency and diet have not been well documented.

children.name: Rhinolophus convexus
children.description: A small Sundaland bat from peninsular Thailand and Malaysia, named for the convex profile of its noseleaf sella. Roosts in limestone caves in small colonies. Very little is known about its diet, echolocation, and reproductive biology.

children.name: Rhinolophus coelophyllus
children.description: A small bat found in Myanmar, Thailand, and Indochina, associated with limestone karst landscape. The species name refers to the hollow or channelled noseleaf. Roosts in caves and emits calls around 78–82 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus formosae
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan (Formosa), this large horseshoe bat inhabits montane forest and roosts in caves and mines. Once considered a subspecies of R. luctus; molecular studies confirmed its species status. Echolocates at approximately 54 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus gordoni
children.description: Known from Afghanistan, Iran, Oman, and Pakistan, this bat inhabits arid and semi-arid rocky terrain. Roosts in caves and mines in small groups. It is morphologically similar to R. clivosus and was long included within that species.

children.name: Rhinolophus imaizumii
children.description: A little-known species from the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, named after Japanese mammalogist Yoshinori Imaizumi. Inhabits subtropical forest and roosts in caves. Closely related to R. pumilus; some authorities treat it as a subspecies.

children.name: Rhinolophus inops
children.description: Endemic to Mindanao and adjacent Philippine islands, this medium-sized bat inhabits lowland and montane forest. Roosts in limestone caves alongside other cave-dwelling bats. Its echolocation frequency and prey preferences are poorly documented.

children.name: Rhinolophus keyensis
children.description: Endemic to the Kai Islands (Kepulauan Kai) in Maluku Province, Indonesia. A poorly known island endemic with very few museum records. The species' relationship to nearby Wallacean Rhinolophus populations is not fully resolved.

children.name: Rhinolophus lepidus
children.description: A small widespread bat from Afghanistan and India east through South and Southeast Asia to southern China. Echolocates at approximately 109–119 kHz, among the highest in the genus. Named after Edward Blyth, curator of the Asiatic Society of Bengal's museum.

children.name: Rhinolophus luctus
children.description: One of the largest Asian horseshoe bats, with dark woolly fur and a striking white ear margin. Distributed from the Himalayan foothills through Southeast Asia to China and Indonesia. Calls at around 52–55 kHz, using low frequency to detect large prey in dense forest.

children.name: Rhinolophus macrotis
children.description: Distributed from Nepal and India through Southeast Asia to the Philippines and southern China, notable for its conspicuously large ears. Inhabits forest and roosts in caves, rock crevices, and buildings. Echolocates at approximately 62–65 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus malayanus
children.description: A medium-sized bat of peninsular Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam, and Laos. Inhabits lowland rainforest and forages at forest edge and in forest interior. Echolocates at around 84–88 kHz and roosts in caves in small colonies.

children.name: Rhinolophus marshalli
children.description: Known from Thailand and adjacent parts of Myanmar and Laos, roosting in caves in karst limestone areas. Named in honour of Joe T. Marshall, an American mammalogist who contributed substantially to knowledge of Southeast Asian bats.

children.name: Rhinolophus megaphyllus
children.description: One of only two horseshoe bat species native to Australia, occurring in coastal eastern Australia from Queensland to Victoria and also in New Guinea. Roosts in caves and mines in colonies that can number thousands. Echolocates at approximately 69–74 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus mehelyi
children.description: A medium-sized bat of southern Europe, North Africa, and Southwest Asia, roosting in large colonies in warm caves. Population is declining sharply due to cave disturbance and pesticide use reducing prey. Named after Hungarian zoologist Lajos Méhely.

children.name: Rhinolophus mitratus
children.description: Known from a handful of specimens from India, this poorly known species is named for the mitre-shaped profile of its noseleaf. Its taxonomy relative to other Indian rhinolophids has been debated; some authorities regard it as a form of R. affinis. Roosts in caves in Bihar and nearby states.

children.name: Rhinolophus monoceros
children.description: An endemic bat of Taiwan, its name meaning 'unicorn' in reference to the single prominent projection on the noseleaf connecting piece. Inhabits lowland and montane forest and roosts in caves, mines, and buildings. Echolocates at around 111 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus paradoxolophus
children.description: A distinctive, large bat of Vietnam, Laos, Thailand, and southern China, with a highly complex noseleaf unlike any other Rhinolophus species. The elaborate noseleaf structure may be used in species recognition during echolocation. Named in part for its paradoxical noseleaf morphology.

children.name: Rhinolophus pearsonii
children.description: A large cave-dwelling bat ranging from India and Nepal through southern China to Vietnam and Myanmar. Emits calls at approximately 55–58 kHz, well suited to detecting large moths in forested hills. Named after H.H. Pearson, an English naturalist.

children.name: Rhinolophus perditus
children.description: Restricted to the Talaud Islands of Indonesia, in the deep-water gap between Sulawesi and the Philippines. Known from very few specimens; its roosting habits, diet, and echolocation have not been described. Listed as Data Deficient by IUCN.

children.name: Rhinolophus philippinensis
children.description: Ranging across the Philippines, New Guinea, and northeastern Australia, this large bat inhabits lowland forest and caves. It is closely related to R. megaphyllus and the two species may co-roost in northern Australia. Echolocates at approximately 58–63 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus pusillus
children.description: One of the smallest horseshoe bats, distributed from India and Nepal across Southeast Asia to China and Japan. Echolocates at 109–120 kHz, among the highest in the genus. Often found roosting in small groups in caves and buildings in both forested and agricultural landscapes.

children.name: Rhinolophus rex
children.description: One of the largest horseshoe bats in the world, known from Guizhou and Guangxi provinces in southern China. Roosts in large, warm caves and emits calls at approximately 43–47 kHz — the lowest frequency recorded in the genus, enabling detection of very large prey.

children.name: Rhinolophus robinsoni
children.description: Restricted to the Thai-Malay Peninsula and closely related to R. stheno. Roosts in limestone cave systems in Peninsular Malaysia and southern Thailand. Named after the English naturalist Herbert C. Robinson.

children.name: Rhinolophus rouxii
children.description: A widespread bat from southern India and Sri Lanka to southern China and Vietnam. Roosts colonially in caves and is one of the commonest horseshoe bats in India. Echolocates at approximately 79–85 kHz and forages for insects over water and in forest undergrowth.

children.name: Rhinolophus rufus
children.description: A large bat endemic to the Philippine archipelago, found on Luzon, Mindanao, and several smaller islands. Inhabits lowland and montane forest and roosts in caves. Its reddish-brown coloration gives it the common name 'rufous'.

children.name: Rhinolophus sedulus
children.description: A poorly known bat restricted to Borneo, where it inhabits lowland dipterocarp forest. The species name 'sedulus' means diligent. Very few specimens exist in museum collections; its echolocation frequency and colony size are undescribed.

children.name: Rhinolophus shameli
children.description: A medium-sized bat from Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam, roosting in caves in limestone karst areas. Named after Herbert D. Shamel, an American mammalogist with the U.S. National Museum. Calls at around 73–77 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus shortridgei
children.description: Known from Myanmar and adjacent parts of Thailand and China. Named after the naturalist Guy Shortridge, who collected extensively in the Indo-Burma region. Roosts in caves; details of its echolocation and diet are largely unknown.

children.name: Rhinolophus siamensis
children.description: A recently described species from Thailand and Myanmar, separated from R. affinis on the basis of echolocation frequency, morphology, and genetics. Roosts in caves in small colonies. Emits calls at approximately 77–80 kHz, lower than sympatric R. affinis.

children.name: Rhinolophus stheno
children.description: A common bat of peninsular Southeast Asia, including Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Vietnam. Roosts in large colonies in caves and emits calls at approximately 85 kHz. Often captured in mist nets over forest streams.

children.name: Rhinolophus subbadius
children.description: A small bat of Nepal, northeastern India, and Myanmar, found at forest edge and in secondary vegetation. One of the few rhinolophids whose distribution is centred on the Himalayan foothills. Echolocates at approximately 97–105 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus subrufus
children.description: An endemic bat of the Philippines found on Luzon and Mindanao, smaller than the closely related R. rufus. Inhabits forest and roosts in caves. Its biology and echolocation remain poorly documented compared to continental relatives.

children.name: Rhinolophus thomasi
children.description: A small bat of Myanmar, China, Thailand, and Vietnam, named after British mammalogist Oldfield Thomas of the Natural History Museum. Roosts in caves and emits calls at approximately 110–116 kHz, among the highest recorded in Southeast Asian Rhinolophus.

children.name: Rhinolophus tiomanensis
children.description: Described from Pulau Tioman off the east coast of peninsular Malaysia, though also found on the Malay Peninsula and in Borneo. Separated from R. stheno on morphological and echolocation grounds. Echolocates at approximately 89–93 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus trifoliatus
children.description: A large, distinctively marked horseshoe bat of Southeast Asia, recognizable by the pale patches behind the ears and the three-lobed sella tip that gives it its name. Distributed from the Andaman Islands through Peninsular Malaysia to Borneo. Emits calls at approximately 56–61 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus virgo
children.description: An uncommon bat of the Philippines known from Leyte, Mindanao, and nearby islands. Its yellowish facial skin and noseleaf are distinctive. Inhabits lowland tropical forest; roost sites and echolocation parameters are inadequately known.

children.name: Rhinolophus yunnanensis
children.description: Described from Yunnan Province in southwestern China; also recorded in Myanmar and Thailand. Inhabits subtropical forest and roosts in limestone caves. Echolocates at approximately 70–75 kHz and is closely related to R. pearsonii.

children.name: Rhinolophus pumilus
children.description: A small bat of the Philippines, Sulawesi, and the Moluccas, sometimes lumped with R. pusillus but recognised as distinct by most recent authorities. Inhabits forest and limestone karst; echolocates at approximately 113–120 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus arcuatus
children.description: A medium-sized bat of the Philippines, Sulawesi, Timor, and New Guinea. Inhabits both lowland and montane forest. Named for the arched (arcuate) connecting process of the noseleaf; echolocates at approximately 76–82 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus canuti
children.description: A small bat known only from Flores and Sumbawa in the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia. Named after the Indonesian naturalist Canut. Very few specimens have been collected and its ecology and echolocation are virtually unknown.

children.name: Rhinolophus creaghi
children.description: A bat of Borneo and the Sulu Archipelago, found in lowland forest and cave systems. Named after Crespigny Creagh, a British colonial administrator in Sabah. Echolocates at approximately 67–72 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus doriae
children.description: A bat restricted to Borneo, occurring in lowland and hill forest up to 1,500 m elevation. Named after Italian naturalist Giacomo Doria, who made important collections in Borneo. Echolocates at approximately 65–72 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus luctoides
children.description: A large bat of the Philippine archipelago, closely related to but distinct from R. luctus of mainland Asia. Inhabits montane forest and roosts in caves and hollow trees. Its echolocation frequency of approximately 50–54 kHz indicates a preference for large aerial prey.

children.name: Rhinolophus petersii
children.description: A poorly known East African species sometimes treated as synonymous with R. clivosus. Described by German naturalist Wilhelm Peters from material collected on the East African coast. Roosts in caves and rock crevices in coastal forest areas.

children.name: Rhinolophus sinicus
children.description: One of the most widespread and abundant horseshoe bats in China, also occurring in Nepal, India, and Southeast Asia. Gained international attention as a known host of SARS-related coronaviruses. Roosts in caves in large colonies and emits calls at approximately 79–84 kHz.

children.name: Rhinolophus cornutus
children.description: A small horseshoe bat endemic to Japan, including the main islands and Ryukyus. Roosts in caves and mines and is one of the commonest Japanese bat species. Echolocates at approximately 109–115 kHz; its high frequency is characteristic of small-bodied rhinolophids.

children.name: Rhinolophus nippon
children.description: Closely related to R. ferrumequinum, this bat is found in Japan, Korea, and northeastern China. Often treated as a subspecies of R. ferrumequinum but increasingly recognised as distinct based on echolocation frequency and genetic data. Roosts in large warm caves and mines.

children.name: Rhinolophus osgoodi
children.description: Known from Yunnan Province in China and northern Myanmar. Named after Wilfred Hudson Osgood, an American zoologist who collected extensively in Asia. Roosts in caves; very few specimens are known and its biology is almost completely unstudied.

children.name: Rhinolophus refulgens
children.description: A small bat described from Myanmar, with glossy reddish-brown fur that gives it its name ('shining'). Long treated as a subspecies of R. affinis; some authorities retain it as a full species. Roosts in caves; its echolocation frequency and ecology are poorly known.

children.name: Rhinolophus rufogenys
children.description: A Philippine endemic with distinctive reddish facial coloration around the cheeks and noseleaf. Found on Luzon and several smaller Philippine islands. Very little biological information is available; the species has been rarely collected.

children.name: Rhinolophus suillus
children.description: A small bat of the Philippines found primarily in Mindanao and the Sulu Archipelago. Named for its rather pig-like noseleaf appearance at certain angles. Inhabits lowland rainforest; ecology and echolocation remain poorly known.

children.name: Rhinolophus tatar
children.description: A recently described species from central Asia, known from Kazakhstan and adjacent countries. Separated from R. ferrumequinum based on genetic and acoustic data; calls at a slightly different frequency of approximately 68–72 kHz. Inhabits rocky steppe landscapes.

children.name: Rhinolophus tylopus
children.description: A bat of peninsular Malaysia and Borneo, found in lowland and peat swamp forest. The species name means 'knob-footed' in reference to a distinctive tarsal feature. Calls at approximately 60–65 kHz and is associated with undisturbed lowland forest.

children.name: Rhinolophus vietus
children.description: A tiny bat described from Vietnam and also recorded in adjacent Laos and China. Among the smallest members of the genus; echolocates at approximately 115–120 kHz. Inhabits limestone karst caves and surrounding tropical forest.

children.name: Rhinolophus adami
children.description: A large horseshoe bat from Central Africa, known from the Congo Basin and adjacent areas. Named after the Belgian biologist Jean-Paul Adam. Roosts in caves and large hollow trees; echolocates at approximately 52–56 kHz, enabling detection of large moths.

children.name: Rhinolophus comorensis
children.description: An island endemic found only in the Comoros Archipelago in the Indian Ocean between Mozambique and Madagascar. Inhabits forest and roosts in caves; its isolation has resulted in morphological divergence from mainland African relatives. Considered Vulnerable due to its tiny range.

children.name: Rhinolophus gorontalensis
children.description: Described from the Gorontalo region of northern Sulawesi, Indonesia. A poorly known Wallacean endemic with very few records. Its noseleaf morphology and echolocation parameters distinguish it from other Sulawesi rhinolophids.

children.name: Rhinolophus nereis
children.description: Known from Bangka Island off southeastern Sumatra, Indonesia. Named after the sea-nymph Nereis of Greek mythology, reflecting the island setting. Roosts in limestone caves; its ecology, echolocation, and population status are virtually unknown.

children.name: Rhinolophus sakejiensis
children.description: Described in 2006 from the Sakeji River area of Zambia, this species was detected acoustically before its morphological description. Emits calls at approximately 97–101 kHz. Inhabits miombo woodland in north-western Zambia and adjacent Democratic Republic of Congo.

children.name: Rhinolophus smithersi
children.description: A poorly known large horseshoe bat described from Zimbabwe and named after the mammalogist Reay Smithers. Roosts in warm caves in miombo woodland. Its relationship to the R. hildebrandtii / R. lobatus complex has been debated.

children.name: Rhinolophus willardi
children.description: A recently described species from Malawi and Zambia, named after the American bat biologist Joe Willard. Separated from R. fumigatus on genetic and acoustic grounds. Echolocates at approximately 50–53 kHz and inhabits miombo woodland.

children.name: Rhinolophus xinanzhongguoensis
children.description: A recently described species from Sichuan and Yunnan provinces in southwestern China, separated from R. affinis on acoustic and genetic grounds. Roosts in limestone caves in subtropical forest. Echolocates at approximately 75–78 kHz, lower than sympatric relatives.

children.name: Rhinolophus bocharicus
children.description: A bat of Central Asia found in Afghanistan, Iran, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan. Inhabits rocky desert and semi-desert terrain, roosting in caves and crevices. Sometimes treated as a subspecies of R. hipposideros but recognised as distinct by most recent authorities.

children.name: Rhinolophus chaseni
children.description: A small bat known from the north coast of Borneo and adjacent Balabac Island. Named after Frederick Nutter Chasen, curator of the Raffles Museum in Singapore. Inhabits lowland forest near limestone; extremely little is known of its ecology.

children.name: Rhinolophus euryotis
children.description: A bat of the Moluccas and New Guinea with notably broad ears for the genus. Inhabits lowland and hill forest; roosts in caves. Its broad ears and relatively low echolocation frequency of approximately 64–68 kHz may improve prey detection in cluttered forest habitats.

children.name: Rhinolophus mixtus
children.description: Known from a small number of specimens from Sulawesi, Indonesia. The species name reflects its intermediate morphological features between other Wallacean Rhinolophus. Almost nothing is known of its ecology, echolocation, or population status.

children.name: Rhinolophus mucus
children.description: A poorly known bat from the Khasi Hills of northeastern India and adjacent Myanmar. Described in the early 20th century and rarely recorded since. Its taxonomic status relative to other northeast Indian rhinolophids is uncertain pending molecular analysis.

children.name: Rhinolophus mabuensis
children.description: Described in 2016 from Mount Mabu, a rainforest 'sky island' in northern Mozambique discovered via Google Earth in 2008. One of several new vertebrate species found in this isolated highland forest. Echolocates at approximately 106 kHz and roosts in forest caves.

children.name: Rhinolophus yatesi
children.description: A newly described species from northern Borneo, separated from related taxa on the basis of echolocation frequency and morphometrics. Inhabits foothill and submontane forest around limestone karst. Its discovery highlights the continuing undescribed bat diversity on Borneo.
